Tafseer:
-1 Word Research and Explanation of Verses 
Ya ayyuhalladhina amanu la tuqaddimu bayna yadayillahi wa rasulihi wattaqullah innallaha sami'un 'alim 1
The Circumstances in Which This Surah Was Revealed 
Although the address is directed to the general Muslims, the people whose conduct is under discussion in this Surah—as will become gradually clear from the upcoming verses—are those Bedouin tribes from the outskirts of Medina. Impressed by the rising power of Islam, they had joined the Muslims, but faith (Iman) had not yet properly settled deep into their hearts. The first reason for this was that these people did not enter Islam by understanding it, but rather because they were overawed by it. Secondly, due to staying disconnected from the center, their training and upbringing had not been carried out properly either. Inside them was also a misplaced vanity that they had accepted the obedience of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) without any war, which they considered a favor upon him. The effect of this vanity was that whenever their chiefs came to Medina, they would speak to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in such a manner as if they were great patrons and benefactors of Islam. Without the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) asking for their opinion on any matter, they would step forward to present their own views and try to give advice. While talking, they would raise their voices above the voice of the Holy Prophet to display their superiority. Whenever they arrived, their desire was that the Prophet should immediately drop all his work and meet with them, and if there was even a slight delay, they would start calling out to him from outside the private quarters without hesitation, the way a common man is called out to. Due to the ignorant rivalries that had persisted among them since the days of ignorance (Jahiliyyah), everyone would try to make the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) their ally. For this purpose, they would sometimes bring news regarding their rivals to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that was misleading. If the Muslims of Medina took any action based on that, it would have been highly detrimental to the collective life of the Muslims.
These were the circumstances in which this Surah was revealed. In this, the behavior under discussion, as we have indicated, belongs to a specific group, but the Quran has kept the address general so that they are not overly disgraced and so that the loopholes through which Satan could find a way to incite mischief within the society are closed.
The Prohibition Is Not Against Offering an Opinion Before the Messenger, but Against Taking the Initiative in Offering an Opinion 
It should be made clear that the prohibition here is against taking the initiative in presenting one's opinion before the Messenger of Allah, or prioritizing one's opinion over the command of Allah and His Messenger, and not against merely presenting an opinion before the Messenger. In matters of public interest, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) used to ask the Companions for their opinions, and the Companions would present their views. Similarly, in general matters of public interest, the Companions would sometimes submit to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that if a certain action of the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was not based on divine revelation (Wahy), then a different strategy would be more appropriate, and the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) would sometimes accept their opinions. This verse does not forbid such interactions. The Prophet himself encouraged this through his own conduct. It is mentioned in some traditions that the Prophet was the one who consulted people the most. From Allah Almighty as well, as is clear from the verse 'and consult them in the matter' (Al-Imran: 159), you were instructed to keep consulting with the people.
The prohibition here is only against what we pointed out above: that a person, considering the Messenger of Allah as an ordinary man or merely a leader, and deeming himself more tactful than him, should try to influence the Prophet with his opinion and prioritize his own view over the Prophet's word without the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) asking for his opinion on the matter. If anyone does this, his attitude is proof that he is completely unaware of the true status and station of the Messenger. The Messenger of Allah is the representative of Allah Almighty, and whatever he says or does, he says or does under the guidance of Allah Almighty. If a person dares to prioritize his own word over his word, it means, in other words, that he wants to prioritize his opinion over the guidance of Allah Almighty, whereas this is something that can demolish his entire faith and deeds, even if he is not conscious of it.
The Affair of Allah and His Messenger Is Not Separate 
From the words 'bayna yadayillahi wa rasulihi' (before Allah and His Messenger), it emerges that the affair of Allah and His Messenger is not separate. The Messenger of Allah is the ambassador and representative of Allah. Giving him unasked advice is like giving advice to Allah Almighty Himself; prioritizing one's own word over his word is prioritizing one's own word over Allah's word, and considering oneself more tactful than him is considering oneself more tactful and wise than the All-Knowing and All-Wise God Himself. These are the inevitable consequences of such a person's attitude. It is possible that a person, due to his foolishness, may not realize these consequences, but it is impossible to deny that these are the inevitable results.
'Wattaqullah innallaha sami'un 'alim' (And fear Allah, indeed Allah is Hearing and Knowing) is a warning to these people to keep fearing Allah and not fall into the delusion of being wiser and more tactful than Allah and His Messenger. Allah is All-Hearing and All-Knowing; He is listening to all your talks and is also well-aware of the motives operating behind them. The meaning is that since He hears and knows everything, His law of retribution will inevitably manifest. The mention of this law is coming up in the following verse.
The Misunderstanding of the Modern-Day Claimants of Reform 
This verse also contains a warning for those people of our time who, while claiming to serve Islam, are distorting its values and altering its laws. Their assumption is that Islam, in the form given by Allah and His Messenger, cannot work in this era. They believe it is necessary to reform it according to the demands of the time. Consequently, they are making amendments to the injunctions of the Shariah according to their own opinions. The only difference is that the verse mentions people who wanted to precede and present their advice ahead of Allah and His Messenger, whereas the claimants of Islam in this era did not get that opportunity, so they are now correcting the mistakes which, according to them—Allah forbid—were made by Allah and His Messenger in the matter of religion.

Ya ayyuhalladhina amanu la tarfa'u aswatakum fawqa sawtin-nabiyyi wa la tajharu lahu bil-qawli ka-jahri ba'dikum li-ba'din an tahbata a'malukum wa antum la tash'urun 2
Those Who Possess Vanity Show Its Impact Through Their Style of Speech 
This points to another aspect of the matter mentioned above. Those people who are obsessed with the idea that they are in a position to advise Allah and His Messenger, or those who harbor the delusion that their acceptance of Islam is a favor upon Islam and the Prophet, could not have a humble and respectful style of address before the Messenger. Instead, it was a natural thing for the impact of this vanity to be evident in their conversation. Consequently, when these people conversed with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it was clear from their style of speech that they had not come to learn anything from the Messenger of Allah, but rather to teach and tell him something. Therefore, just as they took the initiative in presenting their opinions, they also tried to keep their voices louder and higher than the voice of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). If they addressed him, instead of saying 'O Messenger of Allah' out of respect, they would address him by saying 'O Muhammad,' just as one addresses an ordinary man of equal status. Here, they have been forbidden from this uncivilized method of speech and address, because this thing indicated that they had not only failed to recognize the true status and station of the Messenger, but also that the delusion of their own superiority was hidden inside them, which would ultimately wash away all the credit of their Quranic deeds.
In the phrase 'an tahbata a'malukum' (lest your deeds become worthless), a word like 'karahatan' (aversion) or 'makhafatan' (fear) or something synonymous is omitted before it, as we have been clarifying in various places. If you unfold it, the meaning will be: you are being forbidden from this disrespect out of fear that this action of yours might become the cause of all your deeds collapsing in the sight of Allah.
'Wa antum la tash'urun' means you will remain under the delusion that you have rendered a great service to Islam, and that your restlessness to benefit the Prophet with your opinions is out of pure love for serving the religion, but on the other side, all those deeds that you performed for the sake of religion in your own estimation will collapse, and you won't even be conscious of it.
A Highly Important Reality 
From this, a reality becomes clear: a person performs many tasks thinking they are religious works for the service of the religion itself, but if he is obsessed with the vanity that he is doing a favor to Allah and His Messenger or to Allah's religion, and in this delusion, he neither observes the true majesty of Allah Almighty nor respects the real status and station of His Messenger, then all his deeds will go to waste, and he will find out about this unconscious loss only in the Hereafter. Allah Almighty is not dependent on anyone to serve His religion such that a person should start considering himself a benefactor of Allah and His Messenger. In His sight, the honor of acceptance will only be granted to the deeds of those people who serve His religion solely for His pleasure, strictly according to the conditions prescribed by Him, and at the same time remain confessing from their hearts the reality that by performing this service, they have not done any favor to Allah and His Messenger, but rather it is the grace and favor of Allah upon them that He granted them the tawfiq (ability) to render some service to His religion.

Innalladhina yaghudduna aswatahum 'inda rasulillahi ula'ikalladhinamtahanallahu qulubahum lit-taqwa lahum maghfiratun wa ajrun 'azim 3
Teaching of Proper Etiquette Regarding the Messenger 
This is the teaching of the correct etiquette that every believer is required to adopt regarding the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). It is stated that those who lower their voices before the Messenger of Allah are, in reality, the ones whose hearts Allah Almighty has chosen for the cultivation of righteousness (Taqwa). The word 'imtahana' (tested/purified) here implies 'istafa' (chosen) or a synonymous word, from which it emerges that in the sight of Allah Almighty, not every heart is suitable for the sowing and cultivation of Taqwa; rather, for this purpose, Allah Almighty tests and selects hearts. The primary thing that grants preference in this selection is whether a person possesses a true spirit of submission and obedience to Allah and His Messenger and a proper consciousness of humility before them. The more this quality exists in someone, the more he is granted the blessing of Taqwa, and those who are devoid of this consciousness to whatever degree are that much distant from Taqwa. The mention of raising the voice, as we have indicated, has been made as an informant of man's inner state. A person who tries to keep his voice higher than someone else's voice testifies through his action that he considers himself higher than that person. This completely closes the path of acquiring benefit. If a student has this manner of behavior before a teacher, he remains deprived of his benefit. Similarly, if someone adopts this path before the Messenger of Allah, he will be deprived not only of the benefit of the Messenger but also of the tawfiq (guidance) of Allah Almighty, because the Messenger is the representative of Allah Almighty.
The Correct Attitude to Benefit from the Blessings of the Book and Sunnah 
The same status belongs to the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of the Messenger. Allah Almighty selects for Taqwa only those people who adopt this path of humility before His Book and the Sunnah of His Messenger, which was directed regarding the Messenger. The person who has a true spirit of bowing his head before every word of Allah and His Messenger, Allah Almighty opens the paths of Taqwa for him, and guidance comes to him from the unseen at every step. On the other hand, if a person is afflicted with the delusion that he is in a position to reform the Quran and Hadith, then this vanity of his will ruin his entire practice and destroy his Hereafter.
'Lahum maghfiratun wa ajrun 'azim': In the verse above, the consequence of raising one's voice over the voice of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was stated to be a path that destroys deeds. In contrast, this describes the reward for those who keep their voices low before the Messenger. It is stated that for them is forgiveness and a great reward. That is, Allah Almighty will forgive their lapses and shortcomings because they adopted the path of humility before Allah and His Messenger. They did not dare to consider themselves greater than him due to any arrogance. The reward for this humility of theirs was that Allah Almighty selected their hearts for the growth of Taqwa, the reward for which is very great with Allah Almighty.

Innalladhina yunadunaka min wara'il-hujurati aktharuhum la ya'qilun 4
Admonition to the Unintelligent on an Uncivilized Practice 
Just as these people were uncivilized in speaking with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in the assembly, they would also do this: when they saw that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was not present in the assembly, they would immediately start shouting and calling out to him from outside the quarters of the pure wives. This type of action is highly uncivilized in itself, but its inner motive was even more detestable than its outward appearance. These people, as we indicated above and as will be fully explained later, were under the misunderstanding that since they accepted Islam without fighting, this was a huge favor from them upon Islam and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Because of this, they considered it their right that whenever they came, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) should welcome them without delay. If at any time the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was not present in the assembly, they would not tolerate the trouble of waiting; instead, they would immediately go around the quarters of the pure wives and start calling out his name in a very crude manner, shouting out loud. It is stated that most of them do not understand. In the words 'aktharuhum la ya'qilun' (most of them do not understand), there is a reproach for their lack of understanding, and also a subtle hint to overlook their ignorance, implying that although this action of theirs is highly uncivilized, the majority among them consists of those who are neither familiar with the status and station of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) nor with the consequence of this action of theirs, and for this reason, they are in need of training and deserve to be pardoned.
In the phrase 'min wara'il-hujurat', the word 'wara' that has appeared does not necessarily mean that they called out from behind the quarters, making their action objectionable for that reason. The word 'wara' is not exclusive to the meaning of behind or the rear side. In Arabic, the phrase 'nadani min wara'id-dar' simply means that he called out to me from outside the house, regardless of whether he called from behind the house or from the front of the house. The objectionable thing was their calling out in this crude manner. It should be clear that calling out even a common Muslim in this manner is against Islamic etiquette, let alone the Messenger of Allah Almighty. In the commentary of Surah An-Nur, you have already read the method that Allah Almighty preferred for meeting a householder.

Wa law annahum sabaru hatta takhruja ilayhim lakana khayran lahum wallahu ghafurun rahim (5)
They have been instructed with the correct etiquette here: that if they had waited with patience until you came out to them, it would have been a cause of great good and blessing for them! The style of the verse expresses regret over their deprivation. The meaning is that if these people, having reached such a spring of grace, had recognized its true value, they would have returned fully sated. But it is their deprivation that, far from gaining anything from there, they returned empty-handed due to their ignorance and lack of appreciation!
'Wallahu ghafurun rahim': This is Allah Almighty reminding the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) of His attributes of being Forgiving and Merciful. The purpose of this, in a very subtle style, is to draw attention to the reality that although these actions of theirs are highly unpleasant, they are not people of understanding, so for now, overlook these kinds of things from them. Allah Almighty is Forgiving and Merciful, and such forgiveness and overlooking is befitting for His Messenger as well.

Ya ayyuhalladhina amanu in ja'akum fasiqum bi-naba'in fa-tabayyanu an tusibu qawmam bi-jahalatin fa-tusbihu 'ala ma fa'altum nadimin (6)
Emphasis on Caution in Accepting News 
The Muslims of the center (i.e., Medina) are warned here against a political threat coming from such people. Above, we have already indicated that the behavior of some chiefs of the Bedouin tribes around Medina has been described. Due to their lack of training, just as the correct consciousness of the Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) majesty was missing in them, they were also still unfamiliar with the true sense of Islamic brotherhood. When these people came to Medina, some of them would try to make the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) suspicious by giving false and distorted information against their rivals, and they would also win over those Companions upon whom their influence was effective, so that they could use the central power of Medina against their rivals in their own favor. This situation was a delicate one. First of all, the government of Medina was not yet thoroughly consolidated. Secondly, any action taken by it based on such baseless rumor-mongering, especially against a group of Muslims themselves, would be contrary to both justice and collective interest. This situation required that the Muslims of the center be instructed to leave decisions in such important matters entirely to the discretion of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and not try to influence the Prophet's opinion by relying on the reports of untrustworthy people. Consequently, they were instructed that if an untrustworthy person (fasiq) brings news of an important matter, do not take any action by relying on his word without thoroughly investigating the truth of the event, lest you have to regret later over what you did.
By 'fasiq' is meant people who are careless about the limits and boundaries of the Shariah. The investigation of the word 'naba'', as we have explained in its place, means that it refers to an important piece of news, the believing of which or acting upon which has the potential to produce far-reaching consequences. If such important news is brought by someone who is unreliable from a religious and moral standpoint, then both reason and morality demand that his word should not be believed until both the news and the informant are thoroughly investigated. It is possible that the informant gave the news under corrupt motives, and the news is either completely false or has been altered with malicious intent by adding or omitting something to incite passion and provocation in the listeners. The word 'jahalat' here is in the sense of passion and emotional excitement, the investigation of which we have done in various places in this book.

Wa'lamu anna fikum rasulallahi law yuti'ukum fi kathirim minal-amri la-'anittum wa lakinnallaha habbaba ilaykumul-imana wa zayyanahu fi qulubikum wa karraha ilaykumul-kufra wal-fusuqa wal-'isyana ula'ika humur-rashidun, fadlam minallahi wa ni'mah wallahu 'alimun hakim (7-8)
The Correct Attitude 
This is a further emphasis of the same warning: that when the Messenger of Allah is present among you, you should not give so much importance to your opinions and your advice that you try to make the Messenger follow you; rather, you have to follow him. Whatever step he takes, he takes under the guidance of Allah Almighty. For this reason, your success in this world and the Hereafter lies in following him, not in following your passions. If you have to present an opinion, present it respectfully and leave the decision to the discretion of the Messenger; do not desire that your every opinion must necessarily be accepted. Remember well that many of your opinions are flawed. If the Messenger of Allah were to accept all of them, you would fall into great distress. He accepts only those of your opinions that are sound. Thanks to him, you have the guidance of Allah Almighty at every step, so value this blessing and be grateful to your Lord.
The meaning of 'anat' is hardship and distress. 'La-'anittum' means you would fall into great hardship and distress. If a patient, instead of acting upon the physician's discretion, wants the physician to act according to his advice, then it is an obvious matter that such a patient will fall into danger.
Subtleties of the Language 
'Wa lakinnallaha habbaba ilaykumul-imana wa zayyanahu fi qulubikum wa karraha ilaykumul-kufra wal-fusuqa wal-'isyan', meaning: in order to save you from this distress and hardship, Allah Almighty made this special arrangement that He made faith beloved in your eyes and settled it deep into your hearts, and made disbelief, wickedness, and disobedience hateful and detestable in your eyes. The right of this arrangement is that the love and belovedness of faith should remain established and permanent within you, and no stain of disbelief or disobedience should ever fall upon it from any of your words or deeds.
The particle 'ila' after 'habbaba' and 'karraha' points to the special arrangement that Allah Almighty made through His Messenger to make faith beloved and disbelief and wickedness detestable in the eyes of the Companions. In the darkness of the period of ignorance, all values had become completely overturned. Satan had made faith detestable and hateful in the eyes of the people, and disbelief and wickedness beloved and sought after. Faith had become hidden and covered under layers of veils in such a way that tearing them apart to unveil the true beauty and charm of faith for the creation had become equivalent to bringing a river of milk (an impossible task). Similarly, Satan had made disbelief so deceptive with artificial cosmetics that showing its original hideous shape and form to people was equal to crossing seven hazards. It was the favor of Allah Almighty that He sent His Messenger, who, after a long struggle and Jihad, showed faith to the people in its original beautiful form and settled its beauty in their hearts. Similarly, removing the artificial cosmetics from the face of disbelief, he made people familiar with its original detestable and hideous form and made them averse to it. This same subject has been expressed here with the words 'habbaba ila' and 'karraha ila'. That is, both faith and disbelief were presented before you in their true form and shape, due to which you became lovers of faith and averse to disbelief. It is as if both these verbs contain the meaning of presentation, and the letter 'ila' points to it.
Here, as the object of 'habbaba', only faith is mentioned, but with 'karraha', three things are mentioned: disbelief, wickedness, and disobedience. The reason for this is that the people whose character is being commented upon were not yet, as we indicated, well-acquainted with the things that are the opposite of faith. This situation required that it be explained to them clearly that not only is disbelief incompatible with faith, but all matters of wickedness and disobedience also hold the status of the leaves and fruits of the same accursed tree. For this reason, Allah Almighty made them detestable as well.
Although the word 'fisq' is used in the Quran in place of disbelief as well, since it appears here alongside disbelief, it refers to that disobedience of Allah Almighty's command which a person commits while claiming to be a believer. The context and placement of the word 'isyan' here indicate that it has come for the disobedience of the Messenger. Since the disobedience of the caliphs and leaders of the Messenger is also indirectly the disobedience of the Messenger himself, this thing is also included in the meaning of this word.
'Ula'ika humur-rashidun fadlam minallahi wa ni'mah wallahu 'alimun hakim': It is stated that these very people, in whose hearts the beauty of faith has made its home, and who are averse to and free from every taint of disbelief, wickedness, and disobedience, are in reality upon the true guidance. This guidance has been attained by them through the grace of Allah Almighty and His blessing. For this reason, they should remain grateful to their Lord alone for this. No one should fall into the delusion, like the unrefined ones, that he got this thing on his own and has become a benefactor of God and the Messenger. The reference to the attributes of 'Alim' (All-Knowing) and 'Hakim' (All-Wise) is to express the reality that every action of Allah Almighty is based on His knowledge and His wisdom. His distribution is not the distribution of a blind person. He gives the blessing of His religion only to those whom He finds worthy of it.
This verse is in praise of the Muslims of Medina, who regularly benefited from the company of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and were thoroughly dyed in the color of Allah. The context is proof that it also contains a lesson for those unrefined Muslims whose flaws are being commented upon right from the beginning of the Surah, and who belonged to the tribes around Medina.
A Baseless Occasion of Revelation (Shan-i-Nuzul) 
Under Verse 6, our commentators, according to their habit, have also mentioned an occasion of revelation: that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) sent Walid bin Uqbah to Banu Mustaliq for the collection of Zakat. When he reached there, the people of Banu Mustaliq came out in the form of a procession to welcome him. Walid assumed that these people had come out to fight him. Terrified, he immediately returned from there and informed the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that those people had apostatized and refused to pay Zakat! Hearing this news, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) became highly furious with Banu Mustaliq and sent a detachment for their punishment, or decided to send one. In the meantime, the Banu Mustaliq people got the information, and their chief immediately presented himself in Medina and assured the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that they had not even seen the face of Walid, so what was the question of withholding Zakat? After the clarification from their side, their matter was dismissed, but according to our commentators, this verse was revealed based on this report of Walid, and the Muslims were instructed not to take any hasty step relying on the report of a wicked person (fasiq).
Our commentators record some occasion of revelation under almost every verse. Above, under the verse 'Innalladhina yunadunaka...' as well, they have referred to an occasion of revelation, but we did not address it because some critics have questioned it. However, everyone is almost agreed on this occasion of revelation, which is why addressing it is unavoidable.
Regarding the occasion of revelation, always keep in mind that fundamental reality mentioned in the introduction of the commentary: when the predecessors mention an event under a verse as an occasion of revelation, it does not necessarily follow that that exact event was the cause of the revelation of that verse; rather, their meaning by this is that the ruling of this event is also deduced from the verse. This opinion belongs to the experts of the principles of commentary, which is why I have referred to it. Furthermore, it is also known that the traditions regarding the occasions of revelation are mostly weak or even baseless. For this reason, accepting them without testing them on the touchstone of reason and transmission carries the danger of falling into the very mischief from which the believers are forbidden in the verse under discussion.
If you test this occasion of revelation on the touchstone of critical analysis (dirayah), it will become clear that not a single part of it holds up.
The first thing is that the verse forbids relying on the report of a 'fasiq' (wicked/untrustworthy person), whereas before this event, nothing had come before the people regarding Walid from which it could be known—Allah forbid—that he was a 'fasiq'. Not only was there no evidence of his wickedness, but the level of his trustworthiness and integrity was such that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself appointed him to the responsible post of Zakat collection. If there were any such flaw in him, how would the Prophet have selected him for this important service?
The second thing is that if you believe this occasion of revelation, then it must also be accepted that—Allah forbid—the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was so unaware of his Companions that he would appoint such people to responsible positions who, through their fabrication of lies, would put both the government and the subjects in danger. This kind of lack of insight is far-fetched even for an ordinary reasonable man, let alone its issuance from the Leader of the Universe (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).
The third thing is that if Walid had returned out of fear, considering the welcoming party to be a hostile party, and had described his impression before the Prophet that Banu Mustaliq refused to pay Zakat, then this action of his could be attributed to naivety and weakness, but according to the Shariah, it cannot be called 'fisq' (wickedness). Then, a verse of this content should have been revealed: 'O Muslims, do not entrust your responsible positions to such naive people who are incapable of distinguishing between those who welcome and those who fight.' It is worth considering that if Walid were that naive, would the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) have entrusted such an important financial and political responsibility to him? Is naivety inside a person something that appears suddenly out of nowhere, remaining hidden from people to the extent that even the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself could not estimate it!
The fourth thing is that this is the same Walid whom Sayyiduna Uthman Ghani made the governor of Kufah during his caliphate. Reflect: was Hazrat Uthman Ghani not aware of the fact that this person has been declared a 'fasiq' by the text of the Quran, and let alone governorship, according to Islamic law, he is not even eligible to give a report or testimony? If he was unaware, then accept that a Rightly Guided Caliph like Hazrat Uthman, who also holds the honor of being the compiler of the Quran, did not—Allah forbid—possess even as much knowledge of the Quran as those narrators who narrate the traditions of the occasion of revelation had.
I have pointed out only a few aspects of this occasion of revelation; otherwise, inconsistency is present in every aspect of it. In some traditions, it is stated that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had dispatched a punitive detachment. In some, it is that he had decided to dispatch it and had given an ultimatum to Banu Mustaliq that 'if you do not desist from your action, I will send against you a man who is to me like my own self,' while patting the shoulder of Hazrat Ali to encourage him that he would head this mission. In some traditions, contrary to this, it is stated that he sent Hazrat Khalid on this mission. In short, there are as many stories as there are mouths, even though from the phrase 'law yuti'ukum fi kathirim minal-amr', it is clearly known that even if any such matter came before the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), he put it off and the people were warned not to try to influence the Prophet with their opinions.
In my view, this occasion of revelation is among the inventions of the Rawafid (Shia), through which they not only wanted to defame Walid but also tried to malign Hazrat Uthman, claiming that he, despite knowing that this person was a 'fasiq', made him the governor of Kufah merely out of nepotism. Then, during his governorship of Kufah, these oppressors did not leave him alone; rather, they narrated such events of his wickedness that evoke both laughter and tears. Laughter comes at the cleverness of these oppressors, and tears at the simplicity of our commentators who copy these baseless traditions in the books of commentary, even though they do not have even a remote connection with the words of the verse and its context.

Wa in ta'ifatani minal-mu'mininaqtatalu fa-aslihu baynahuma fa-in baghat ihdahuma 'alal-ukhra fa-qatilullati tabghi hatta tafi'a ila amrillahi fa-in fa'at fa-aslihu baynahuma bil-'adli wa aqsitu innallaha yuhibbul-muqsitin 9
It should be made clear that Hazrat Walid was also a relative of Sayyiduna Uthman Ghani.
If a Conflict Occurs Between Two Groups of Muslims, the Responsibility of Other Muslims 
In the verse above, it was forbidden to rely on the report of a 'fasiq', making it unlawful for Muslims to take any action against any group of Muslims based on it. Now it is being told what attitude other Muslims or their government should adopt if two groups of Muslims fight among themselves. It is stated: try to make peace between them. If one of the two parties is not willing to reconcile, or after reconciliation, commits transgression against the other party contrary to the terms of peace, then in that case, the other Muslims or their government must fight the transgressing party until it is forced to bow before the truth.
By 'tafi'a ila amrillah' (returns to the command of Allah) is meant bowing before the decision that the peacemakers have placed before the parties. If any party chooses to evade this reconciliation, it is, as it were, evading bowing before the command of Allah Almighty, because to deal with this situation, Allah Almighty has commanded this very thing, and when Allah has commanded it, its status is that of the Command of Allah (Amr Allah).
'Fa-in fa'at fa-aslihu baynahuma bil-'adli wa aqsitu', meaning: after this collective action of the Muslims, if that party bows before the decision, then no further action will be taken against it on the grounds that it adopted the path of rebellion; rather, peace will be made between the parties according to the demands of justice. Whichever party suffered a loss, its compensation will be properly arranged.
The word 'aqsitu' (be just) has come to emphasize fulfilling the demands of this justice. The meaning is that neither should undue favor be shown to anyone nor should anyone be suppressed contrary to justice; rather, without any bias, whatever the demand of justice is, it must be fulfilled. Allah Almighty loves those who act justly.
Some Collective Principles Derived from the Verse 
If two groups of Muslims fight among themselves, other Muslims should neither consider it a stranger's quarrel and stay completely detached from it, nor is it lawful for them to become the supporters of one and opponents of another merely in the passion of familial, tribal, or group partisanship without investigating who is on the right and who is on the wrong. Instead, they must understand the whole state of the matter and try to make peace between the parties.
If one party does not agree to reconciliation but rather insists on war, or arbitrarily presents such conditions for reconciliation that are contrary to justice, then in that case, it will be the duty of the Muslims to use force against it to compel it to bow before the terms of peace.
In such disputes, the conditions that unbiased Muslims fix for reconciliation by keeping in view the instructions of Allah and His Messenger and the demands of justice, obedience to them will be mandatory upon the parties just as obedience to the injunctions of the Shariah is mandatory, to the extent that the party that deviates from it will be fought against.
After reconciliation is achieved, if any party commits transgression against the other party contrary to its terms, it will be declared the transgressor. It will be the duty of the Muslims to suppress it.
A Difficulty of the Modern Age 
It should be made clear that these instructions have been given for the situation when the dispute occurs between two groups of Muslims and a central power among them is in a position to intervene between the parties. In this era, this complex situation has arisen that many small and large Muslim governments have become established separately. If, God forbid, a war breaks out between them, it is not lawful for other Muslim governments to remain completely detached from this issue. The effort for reconciliation, which has been commanded in the verse, will have to be made by everyone, though practically, the matter of intervention depends on the situation, which is related to the political demands of the time. If the situation permits, it will be completely lawful to use force against the transgressing party to make it bow before the truth. If there is a fear of creating further inter-communal or international complexities, then practical intervention will be avoided, but evading the struggle for reconciliation is not lawful under any circumstance.

Innamal-mu'minuna ikhwatun fa-aslihu bayna akhawaykum wattaqullaha la'allakum turhamun 10
Meaning: Muslims are brothers to one another. First of all, the outbreak of any dispute among them is contrary to their mutual brotherhood. But if any dispute does break out due to the instigation of Satan, then other Muslims must try to make peace between them rather than fanning this fire further. 'Wattaqullaha la'allakum turhamun', meaning: keep fearing Allah Almighty. If any such deed was committed by your hands that became the cause of bloodshed and killing between brothers and brothers, or if you became participants in this bloodshed from any aspect merely for the sake of national, tribal, regional, or political interests, then remember that you will not be able to escape the grasp of Allah. Those who deserve the mercy of Allah Almighty will only be those who always try to keep this brotherhood established by Him strong and permanent, neither creating any breach in it themselves nor giving anyone an opportunity to create any breach in it to the extent of their capability.
In 'bayna akhawaykum' (between your two brothers), the dual form does not make it necessary that the war must be between only two individual brothers; rather, this dual form has been used for two groups of Muslims. Using the dual form in this manner is well-known in Arabic. Examples of this have passed behind.
-2 The Source of Jarh and Ta'dil (Critique and Validation of Narrators) in the Quran 
Our scholars of Hadith (Muhaddithin) and the masters of the science of narrators (Fan-i-Rijal) have made this very verse of Surah Al-Hujurat—'in ja'akum fasiqum bi-naba'in fa-tabayyanu'—the source for the ruling of critique and scrutiny of narrators, thanks to which the magnificent science of Asma al-Rijal came into existence, which is among those sciences whose honor of being the founders belongs first of all to the Muslims in the world.
In the verse, Muslims are commanded that when a 'fasiq' brings news of an important event, investigate it thoroughly. This command, though general, makes the investigation of every such news necessary that carries far-reaching consequences. But if a person narrates something by attributing it to the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), then its investigation and critique are necessary to a greater degree. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is the divinely appointed guide whose obedience is mandatory. His every word, rather his every gesture, holds the status of an excellent model (Uswah Hasanah) for the Ummah. If an incorrect report is made by attributing it to the person of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and it is accepted without investigation, then this thing can become a cause of loss in this world as well as in the Hereafter.
In the verse, although the command to investigate the report is specified for a 'fasiq', from this, it automatically emerges that if a narrator is unknown (majhool)—neither his wickedness is known nor his trustworthiness—then his investigation will also be necessary, because in accepting the report of an unknown narrator, there is a fear that the narrator might possibly be a 'fasiq'. Consequently, the Muhaddithin thoroughly investigated unknown narrators as well so that their wickedness or integrity could become clear. If they did not succeed in the investigation of a narrator, they declared him 'majhool' and rejected his report.
From the verse, it also emerges that this investigation will be necessary only when the 'fasiq' narrates such a report that carries far-reaching consequences, because here the word 'naba'' has been used, which comes only for news that is important and carries far-reaching consequences. This word does not come for ordinary news or events. Consequently, in the ordinary matters of daily life, there is no harm in accepting the news of a 'fasiq' or a disbeliever.
In the verse, the command to investigate has been given for both the narrator and the report, because it has been said: 'O you who believe, if a corrupt person brings you an important piece of news, verify it.' It is obvious that here 'tabayyanu' (verify) applies to the narrator as an object as well as to the report; rather, its application to the report is more obvious because the wickedness of the narrator is already known here. In the investigation of any report, just as the trustworthiness, integrity, and deep understanding (fiqahat) of the narrator carry importance, similarly, the words of the report themselves, its context, its contradiction or agreement with other reports of the chapter, its status on the touchstone of reason and transmission, and most of all, its harmony with the Book of God and other things of this sort carry much more importance than that. If one contents himself only with the investigation of the narrator and ignores these things, then the right of investigation cannot be fulfilled. Our Muhaddithin spend more effort only on the investigation of the narrator; they do not give much importance to reflecting on the text itself from those aspects to which we have pointed. However, without investigating from these aspects, the right of investigation, as we submitted, can never be fulfilled. Consequently, this is the reason why our jurists (Fuqaha) formulated principles for reflecting on the text of Hadith and named it 'dirayah' (critical analysis of the text). In this special service, the greatest share belongs to Hazrat Imam Abu Hanifah (may Allah have mercy on him). By performing this service, he did not just do a favor to jurisprudence alone, but rendered a very great service to the science of Hadith as well. If our scholars had been granted the tawfiq to use these principles correctly, that mischief against Hadith could never have arisen which the mischief-makers raised and which opened many paths for astray sects to infiltrate the religion. If Allah Almighty grants me the tawfiq and time to write my intended book on 'Tadabbur-i-Hadith', I will, inshallah, clarify the value and worth of these principles in its introduction.
From the details above, it became clear that the exposure that our noble Muhaddithin carried out regarding wicked and unknown narrators was done in compliance with this clear and decisive command of the Quran. But in this era, some optimistic gentlemen have come up with this point: that opening the faults of narrators is backbiting (Ghibah), which the Quran declared unlawful in Verse 12 of this very Surah and termed it as eating the flesh of one's dead brother; however, the Muhaddithin made this unlawful thing lawful under strategy so that they could save the religion from the reports of wicked narrators. Then, from this point, those gentlemen derived another point even more deep and subtle: that all prohibitions of the Shariah are not eternal, and for this reason, a leader of an Islamic movement also has the right to change a prohibition into lawfulness whenever he feels the need under the demands of strategy. We have already expressed our humble thoughts on these points in our essays. There is neither a need nor space to critique them here. Just remember this much: when the Quran itself gave the command for the exposure of wicked and unknown narrators and the investigation of their reports with this clarity, why would the Muhaddithin take the trouble to make an unclean thing like backbiting lawful under strategy for this service?
It is also worth remembering here that the credit for declaring the critique on narrators as backbiting goes to our masters of Sufism (Tasawwuf). Since the entire structure of Sufism is established only on weak and baseless reports, when the Muhaddithin started the work of scrutinizing narrators, these gentlemen felt that if the Muhaddithin continued to do this work with this fearlessness, the entire structure of Sufism would fall to the ground. To save Sufism from this danger, these gentlemen came up with this point: that these Muhaddith gentlemen go around backbiting people. This point of the Sufis became very popular within their circles. This very point of theirs has been adopted by some minds of our era for their own strategy, and based on it, they formulated such a principle that would completely undo the entire religion.
In the end, remember one more thing regarding this verse. Some Muhaddithin and Fuqaha give importance to the practical wickedness (fisq-i-amali) of a 'fasiq' in the chapter of accepting his report, but they do not give much importance to his doctrinal wickedness (fisq-i-aqa'idi). Their opinion is that if a person is involved in some practical wickedness, for example, lying and bad conduct, etc., then his report or testimony will certainly not be accepted; but if he is merely involved in some wicked belief, then his report or testimony will not be rejected merely on the basis of the corruption of his belief. In our view, this opinion is completely incorrect. Experience is witness that the number of false reports fabricated by narrators involved in corrupt beliefs is far greater than those fabricated by narrators involved in corrupt practices. It is their fabricated reports that are filled in the books of biography (Sirat), commentary (Tafseer), Sufism, and history, and with which the people of innovation and misguidance have decorated their shops. May Allah Almighty have mercy on those scholars who, by giving this relaxation to narrators of corrupt beliefs, faced the Muslim nation with such a mischief that dealing with it became a highly difficult task for the people of truth.
In our view, this division into practical wickedness and doctrinal wickedness is meaningless. There is no evidence present in its favor in the Quran and Hadith. The general formula stated above about general wicked people is also in accordance with reason and transmission regarding narrators of corrupt beliefs: meaning, their report and testimony will be accepted in those matters in which the report of a disbeliever can also be accepted, but there is no room to accept their report or testimony in important matters, especially the matters of religion.

-3 The Subject of the Upcoming Verses 11-12 
Above, in Verse 7, Allah Almighty mentioned His special arrangement which He made out of His special grace to save Muslims from disbelief, wickedness, and disobedience. Now, ahead, they are forbidden from some of those things that are contrary to faith and fall under wickedness, and from which the seeds of that corruption are sown inside the hearts which keeps the entire society poisoned. If it is not prevented, then those whom Allah Almighty distinguished with the description of 'rahama'u baynahum' (merciful among themselves) become enemies of one another. Please recite the verses:
-4 Word Research and Explanation of Verses 
Ya ayyuhalladhina amanu la yaskhar qawmum min qawmin 'asa an yakunu khayram minhum wa la nisa'um min nisa'in 'asa an yakunna khayram minhunn wa la talmizu anfusakum wa la tanabazu bil-alqab bi'sa-l-ismul-fusuqu ba'dal-iman wa mal lam yatub fa-ula'ika humuz-zalimun 11
Emphasis on Abstaining from Things Contrary to Faith 
The address 'Ya ayyuhalladhina amanu' (O you who believe) here is not merely for addressing; rather, ahead those evils are being described which fall under wickedness and are contrary to faith. Through this address, the attention of the people of faith has been drawn, as it were, to the reality that it does not behoove those who have been honored with faith to pollute their skirts with the stains of wickedness after faith.
It is stated that after entering faith, it does not behoove men to consider other men inferior and make fun of them, nor is it lawful for women to make other women the target of mockery. In the sight of Allah Almighty, honor and vileness depend on man's faith and deeds, and the correct weight of faith and deeds will be known on the Day of Resurrection through the scale of justice of Allah Almighty. It is possible that a person considers himself a very big thing, but on the Day of Resurrection, it will open up that in the scale of God, his weight is not even equal to a straw. Similarly, there is a possibility of this as well: that the one whom the people of the world never gave a place in their eyes, on the Day of Resurrection it becomes known that the station he has in the kingdom of God is not possessed by those who considered him inferior.
Here, along with men, women have also been mentioned with special arrangement, although apparently there was no need for it. The general words 'la yaskhar qawmum min qawmin' were sufficient for them too. But the Quran has observed this style in the description of both virtues and vices: that the mention of women has occurred with special arrangement in those places where it is intended to urge them with emphasis toward some virtue or to save them from some mischief. Here, it is this second case. The evil from which men are being stopped here is found inside women no less, if not more, than it is found inside men. Those women who possess pride in their family, lineage, and wealth, or in their outward beauty and charm, their style of address and speech with those women whom they consider inferior in comparison to themselves is contemptuous.
The things from which they are stopped here possess a specific inner aspect, and the purpose is in reality the uprooting of that. The last verse of the section points to this inner aspect. Among the great mischiefs that Satan invented to misguide the children of Adam is the mischief of the honor and distinction of lineage, descent, family, brotherhood, household, and tribe. Those who are involved in this mischief (and very few such fortunate ones emerge who can keep themselves safe from this mischief), it is obvious that they consider others inferior in comparison to themselves, and when they consider them inferior, it is inevitably expressed through their words, deeds, and attitude, to the extent that these things become solidified and acquire the status of a tradition among them; rather, if they can, they even give them the status of religion. Consequently, among the Hindus, the Brahmins, among the Jews, the Banu Lavi, and among the Arabs, the Quraysh, created such a position of sanctity for themselves in this manner that challenging it did not remain possible for others. This same has been the state of every nation, and despite the loud claims of human equality, it is the same even today. To the extent that Muslims, who were raised for the uprooting of this mischief, are also divided today into who knows how many brotherhoods, nations, and tribes, and everyone is intoxicated with the pride of 'there is no one like me,' which keeps manifesting through the statements and slogans of the common and special people of every nation and tribe, due to which hatred and malice are naturally created in the hearts against one another, which, taking the shape of enmity and transgression, ultimately brings the matter to bloodshed, division, and separation.
Here, the Quran instructed the Muslims to stay safe from this calamity: that Allah has, out of His grace, taken you out from the darkness of ignorance and granted you the light of faith. Your society is established on the foundation of 'Innamal-mu'minuna ikhwatun' and you have been made 'rahama'u baynahum' among yourselves, so do not try to distort the face of this society by considering your other brothers inferior or making them the target of your sarcastic and contemptuous words.
'Asa an yakunu khayram minhum': This points to that original wisdom which gives correct guidance in the matter that the foundation of honor and nobility is not on lineage, descent, family, brotherhood, nation, tribe, and wealth, but on man's religion and Taqwa. The decision of this matter will take place tomorrow on the Day of Resurrection as to whose Taqwa is greater and who is noble and high in the sight of Allah, and who, despite all his claims of lineage and pride of honor, is completely weightless and realityless in the sight of God.
Prohibition of Sarcasm and Taunting 
'Wa la talmizu anfusakum': The meaning of 'lamz' is to find fault with someone, to cast a sarcastic phrase at him while gesturing with the eyes. For example, in Surah At-Tawbah, Verse 79, it is said about the hypocrites: 'walladhina yalmizuna-l-muttawwi'ina minal-mu'minin', meaning when poor Muslims spend something in the way of Allah out of their hard-earned income, the hypocrites cast sarcastic phrases at them in a manner of lowliness to discourage them, saying, 'Look, they have also stood up to make a display today.' These kinds of poisonous phrases demonstrate the descent of the speaker as well as his pride and arrogance, and their effect on others appears either in the form of discouragement or in the form of hatred and enmity, and both these things spread poison inside the society.
'Anfusakum' (yourselves) has been used here in the same manner as 'la taqtulu anfusakum' (do not kill yourselves) appeared in An-Nisa, Verse 29. From this, it emerged that the Muslim who finds fault with another Muslim, it is as if he finds fault with his own self, because all Muslims are brothers to one another on the principle of 'Innamal-mu'minuna ikhwatun'. So whoever made his brother the target of his fault-finding and sarcasm, he, as it were, made his own chest the target of his arrow and wounded himself.
'Wa la tanabazu bil-alqab': The meaning of 'tanabazu bil-alqab' is to attach bad nicknames to one another. Just as titles of honor enhance the respect of an individual or nation, similarly, attaching bad nicknames to someone is his extreme insult and humiliation. Moreover, these nicknames easily catch on the tongues of the people, and their effect is highly far-reaching and highly durable. The bitterness created by them persists for generations, and if this taste develops so much in a society that the poets, writers, editors, and leaders of every group employ their intelligence in inventing bad nicknames for their rivals, then there is no good left for that nation. Its unity is bound to be torn to pieces. It should be kept in mind here that during the period of ignorance, this taste was at the peak of perfection among the Arabs. The greatest poet and orator of a tribe was considered to be the one who was unique in describing the glories of his tribe in comparison to others and in the satire and disparagement of rivals. Read their satirical verses to get an idea of what a prominent position they had achieved in this noble art. This thing of theirs never let them become one nation. They remained regularly engaged in pulling down and knocking down their own people. For the first time in history, Islam made them familiar with human unity and faith-based harmony, thanks to which they became worthy of the guidance and leadership of the world. The Quran has warned them here against these very mischiefs of the period of ignorance: that Allah Almighty has blessed you with the blessings of faith and Islam, so value it. Do not fall again due to the enticement of Satan into those boasts and dust-playings from which Allah saved you.
'Bi'sa-l-ismul-fusuqu ba'dal-iman': The concept of exaggeration is found to some extent within 'bi'sa' (wretched is) and 'ni'ma' (excellent is). Its exact meaningful translation will be: 'Extremely bad is the word wickedness after faith.' This is similar to saying somewhere 'the name of the wicked is bad, then what to speak of the badness of being wicked!' In our language as well, this style exists to express the extreme badness of a thing. For example, they say, 'Brother, one feels disgust even from the name of this thing.'
Through the addition of 'ba'dal-iman' (after faith), it is intended to point to this reality: if you had not been familiar with faith and some matter of the category of wickedness had been issued from you, then this thing would not be very surprising. But when Allah made you familiar with the sweetness of faith, as He stated: 'and He made disbelief, wickedness, and disobedience detestable to you,' then now you should feel disgust even at the name of wickedness.
Let alone that some act of wickedness was issued from you.
From this piece, this reality became clear that the things from which they are stopped above are all included in wickedness, and the faith-based sensitivity of the people of faith should be so awake that far from committing wickedness, they should be averse to and fed up with even the word wickedness.
'Wa mal lam yatub fa-ula'ika humuz-zalimun': This is a warning in very severe words to those who will commit any such wickedness even after this clarification. It is stated that those who do not repent from these things, let them remember that they will be the wrongdoers. The meaning is that Allah Almighty has clarified the blessings of faith before the people and has also made them well-aware of the consequences of disbelief and wickedness. Now the responsibility is the people's own. Even after this completion of proof (itmaam-i-hujjat), those who do not desist from their path will inevitably face its consequences, and this will not be any wrongdoing upon them from Allah Almighty, but they themselves will be the ones inflicting wrongdoing upon their own souls.
Some Prohibitions Which Are Apparently Small but Big in Terms of Their Inner Aspect

Ya ayyuhalladhina amanu-jtanibu kathiram minaz-zanni inna ba'daz-zanni ithmun wa la tajassasu wa la yaghtab ba'dukum ba'da a-yuhibbu ahadukum an ya'kula lahma akhihi maytan fa-karihtumuh wattaqullah innallaha tawwabur-rahim 12
Addressing the people of faith anew, they are forbidden here from some such things which apparently seem ordinary but afflict man's own heart with such a disease that it becomes completely unsuitable for the growth of Taqwa. For this reason, for those to whom faith is dear, it is necessary to keep themselves safe from these calamities.
Suspicion (Badgimani) 
The first thing stated is that man should not make his heart a breeding ground for suspicions regarding others, storing away in some corner whatever bad assumption arises in the heart about someone. It is a natural thing for a good or bad assumption to arise in the heart regarding those with whom one has to deal in life. This very assumption connects or breaks man from man; from this aspect, it is the foundation of connection and separation in society. This importance of it demands that man should not be careless and easy-going in the matter of its rejection and acceptance, but rather highly alert and vigilant. Islam has given this guidance to the people of faith in this chapter: that a Muslim should always hold a good assumption (husn-i-zann) about another Muslim, unless it is proven that he is not deserving of this good assumption. This good assumption is an essential requirement of that faith-based brotherhood upon which Islam established the foundation of society and the explanation of which has passed above. If a person, contrary to this, fixes the principle that he keeps storing all the raw and dry assumptions that arise in his heart, then the example of such a lover of assumptions is like that hunter who becomes so blind in his passion for catching fish that while catching fish, he catches a snake as well. It is obvious that the person who becomes so blind in his passion for fish carries the danger that one day he might lose his life in this very passion. The Quran has stopped the Muslims here from this very danger: do not pursue assumptions too much, because some assumptions are clear sins that throw man into destruction. From this emerged this teaching that a believer should not become a patient of suspicions, but should hold a good opinion of his other brothers. If something is issued from someone that causes suspicion, then as far as possible, interpret it well if any good interpretation can come out. Adopting its bad aspect is lawful only when no good interpretation can emerge from it. If a man holds a favorable opinion of someone who deserves suspicion, this is lighter in comparison to holding a suspicion against someone who deserves a favorable opinion. In the noble Hadith, the definition of a believer has come as: 'Al-mu'minu gharrun karim' (The believer is simple and noble). In this era, the generally preferred principle of the people is to hold a suspicion against everyone unless he proves that he is a man of their sort. People consider this thing as politics and cleverness. Against the enemy, this cleverness and alertness is necessary; under 'ashidda'u 'alal-kuffar' (severe against the disbelievers), we have already explained it. But how can this politics be correct against the people of faith, when the Quran instructed them to be 'adhillatan 'alal-mu'minin' (humble toward the believers) and 'rahama'u baynahum' (merciful among themselves)?
Spying (Tajassus) 
The second thing stated in the verse is 'wa la tajassasu' (and do not spy on one another). Just as in the piece above, they were stopped not from good assumptions but from bad assumptions, similarly, the prohibition here is against engaging in spying that is with a bad purpose. Meaning, the search should be for the purpose that something regarding the private life of another comes to hand, through which awareness of his flaws and access to the secrets of his household can be gained. This thing sometimes arises from the passion of jealousy: that some such aspect of the rival's life comes forward through which the heart is comforted. Sometimes the intensity of malice and enmity is its cause: that some such thing comes to hand through the publication of which the opponent can be disgraced when needed. In this era, it has also taken the shape of a profession, which modern journalism has advanced a lot. Some journalists go around day and night in search of some scandal, and among them, the shrewdest journalist is considered to be the one who succeeds in finding some such scandal regarding the private life of a prominent personality due to which his newspaper or magazine sells hand-to-hand. This kind of spying is obviously completely contrary to that brotherhood and mutual sympathy which is the foundation of Islamic society, which is why the people of faith have been stopped from it. As for that spying which a Muslim does regarding the states of his other Muslim brother with the purpose that he can lend a hand in his difficulties and needs, or an Islamic government does with the purpose that it stays fully aware of the states of the subjects, then this spying is neither under discussion here nor is it forbidden; rather, it is a highly righteous work for every noble neighbor to be aware of the states and problems of his neighbors so that he can help them in their difficulties, and for the government, it is not just righteousness but its duty to make arrangements to be fully aware of both the good and bad states of the subjects so that it can discharge its responsibilities properly.
Backbiting (Ghibah) 
The third thing stated is 'wa la yaghtab ba'dukum ba'da' (and let not some of you backbite others). The meaning of backbiting is to describe someone's fault behind his back. Within the concept of 'behind the back' itself is included this matter: that the backbiter wants the information of this action of his not to reach the one whose faults he is describing. Due to this very desire, he does this work behind his back only before those people who are either his confidants, like-minded, and partners in purpose, or at least there is no fear from them that they will be his sympathizers whose faults he is describing and will disclose this secret before him. This very feature of backbiting makes it a highly detestable and hideous action, because through it, neither the purpose of supporting any truth is achieved nor can any reform be expected; rather, in this manner, a cowardly person merely tries to vent the grudge of his heart against someone.
Some optimistic people declare every form of mentioning someone's fault as backbiting. According to them, the Muhaddithin critiquing narrators, testifying against someone in court, forbidding an evil from an evildoer, registering a report against someone at the police station, informing someone seeking advice regarding some real fault of a person, and all such things fall under backbiting, but these backbitings have been made lawful under strategy. Then, right from here, they derive a legal principle for themselves that all prohibitions of the Shariah are not eternal, which is why they also have the right that if their strategy requires making a prohibition permissible, they can declare it lawful. In our view, this is a highly severe kind of mischief against the religion, from which the doors of many new mischiefs can open. For this reason, we have written independent articles in its refutation, and here too, we will throw light on some of its aspects in an independent section ahead.
'A-yuhibbu ahadukum an ya'kula lahma akhihi maytan fa-karihtumuh': This clarifies the hideousness of backbiting through an example: that the person who describes the fault of his brother behind his back, he, as it were, is eating his flesh in that state when he is lying dead and is completely incapable of his own defense. It is stated that this thing is such that none of you likes it, so when you are not ready to tolerate it, why do you tolerate a similar detestable thing, backbiting? 'Maytan' (dead) here appears as a state (haal) from the mudaf in 'akhihi', and this is a picture of his helplessness in his own defense.
'Wattaqullaha innallaha tawwabur-rahim': This is a warning as well as an encouragement for repentance and reform of state. It is stated: fear Allah. Those who become habituated to eating the flesh of their brothers for free in this manner develop such a taste for it that they lose their faith itself behind it. Allah has warned you in time so that through repentance and reform, you can keep yourself safe from this danger. If you repent, Allah is highly Accepting of repentance and Merciful to His servants.
If you cast a look of reflection on the six things from which they are stopped in these two verses (11-12), it will be known that among them, the upper three things—making fun, finding fault, and attaching bad nicknames—are among those evils which man commits openly in public. The remaining three evils—bad assumption, spying, and backbiting—belong to man's private life, which he commits by hiding them from others or keeping them limited within his confidants. The prohibition of both these kinds of evils is established on that principle of Islamic purification and cleansing which has been described in the Quran with the words: 'wa dharu zahiral-ithmi wa batinah' (Al-An'am: 120) (and leave the outward sin and its inward aspect). Until man cleanses himself from those evils that belong to his inner self, that consciousness of Allah Almighty being the Knower of the Unseen does not become firmly established inside him, without which the growth of Taqwa inside the heart is completely out of the question.

Ya ayyuhannasu inna khalaqnakum min dhakariw-wa untha wa ja'alnakum shu'ubaw-wa qaba'ila li-ta'arafu inna akramakum 'indallahi atqakum innallaha 'alimun khabir 13
Blow to Lineage and Family Pride 
Through a general address, this puts an immediate end to that lineage, family, and tribal pride which becomes the cause of most of those evils that have been described above. It is stated: O people! Keep this reality well-seated in your minds that We created all of you from a single male and a single female. Meaning, the beginning of the entire human race took place from Adam and Eve alone; for this reason, in terms of creation, no one possesses any honor or superiority over another. The division into families and tribes is merely for introduction and recognition; Allah Almighty did not bless any specific family or tribe with this distinction by itself that whoever is born in it becomes honored in the sight of Allah and starts considering himself noble and high in comparison to others. Just as Allah kept a difference in the faces of the people, their colors, and their statures so that people could recognize one another, similarly, He established the boundaries of families and tribes so that people could recognize one another. These boundaries do not possess any importance more than this. People of any family or tribe should not fall into the vanity that they are honored in the sight of Allah, or that He has blessed them with some superiority over others. In the sight of Allah, the foundation of honor is on Taqwa. In His sight, the most honored among you is the one who fears Him the most and keeps the best observance of His limits.
'Innallaha 'alimun khabir': Meaning, in testing the people on this prescribed standard, there is no possibility of any trouble or any illusion facing Allah Almighty. He is the Knower of everything and is aware of every word and deed of everyone. The one who is deserving of honor will surely get his station of honor, even if he has risen from within a highly unknown and humble family; and the one who is not deserving of it, regardless of how great a Qurashi, Hashimi, Suryavanshi, or Chandravanshi he may be, Allah Almighty will throw him into the very pit of which he is deserving.
5. Explanation of Some Instructions of This Collection of Verses 
We have been providing the necessary explanation under the verses for the matters described in this collection of verses, but some aspects of spying and backbiting are in need of further explanation. Here we want to clear them up as well.
Is Spying on the States of the People Not Lawful for the Government Either? 
In this era, since the concept of individual freedom is highly dominant over minds, some people understand that just as Islam stopped common individuals from spying on the states of others, similarly, it is not lawful for the government either to spy on the states of the people. It should limit its accountability only to those matters that come to its notice openly. As for the matters of the household, they are outside the sphere of accountability of the government. In its support, some people have also referred to an event of Hazrat Umar: that once at night, you heard the sound of a person's singing who was singing in his house. Suspicion crossed your mind, so you climbed the wall. You saw that wine was present there, and a woman too. You called out and said, 'O enemy of God, did you assume that you would disobey Allah and Allah would not expose your veil?' He replied, 'O Emir of the Believers, do not hurry. If I have committed one sin, you have committed three sins together. Allah had forbidden spying, and you spied. Allah commanded to enter houses from their doors, and you came by climbing the wall. Allah commanded not to enter the houses of others without permission, and you entered my house without my permission.' Hearing this reply, Hazrat Umar admitted his mistake and took no action against him, though he took a promise that he would adopt the path of goodness.
This tradition has been recorded by some gentlemen in books of the nature of noble morals and encouragement and warning, but neither does it have any status in terms of transmission (sanad) nor is it reliable from the aspect of the text (matn) itself. For the weakness of the transmission, this much is sufficient: that many people, especially the masters of Sufism, do not believe in the importance of investigating the transmission in traditions of morals and exhortation. Whatever kind of stories yield a useful lesson according to them, they record them open-heartedly without investigating the transmission and text in their books. As for its text, reflect on it and it will be known that this event is completely far-fetched from reason and analogy.
First of all, it is itself un-analogous that any person could have such daring in Medina Munawwarah, and that too during the caliphate of Hazrat Umar, to be engaged in revelries with a lover and wine in such a manner that the sound of singing is heard by Hazrat Umar outside in the streets, and he has to become an intruder in this assembly of luxury. If even at the very center of Islam during the era of the Farooq-i-Azam, the daring of Satan remained in this state, then it must be accepted that Hazrat Umar too could not overawe Satan, even though the statement of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is: 'Umar does not pass through a path except that Satan leaves that path and moves away.'
The second thing is that if you believe this tradition, then it must be accepted that Hazrat Umar was not aware of these commands of the Quran and Hadith that entering someone's house by leaping over his wall is not lawful; rather, one should stand at the door, say salam, and seek permission. If permission is granted, one should enter; otherwise, saying salam three times, one should quietly return on his heels. Can any person possessing reason and sense believe that Hazrat Umar was informed of these clear commands of the Book and Sunnah for the first time by a profligate lover! Did Hazrat Umar never get the opportunity—Allah forbid—to recite Surah An-Nur and Surah Al-Hujurat?
The third thing is that if Hazrat Umar had accepted these three mistakes of his with great righteousness, why did he not issue this directive to his workers and governors: 'Until now I was in error that I used to spy on the houses of the people; now it has become clear to me that three matters in it are contrary to the Shariah, for which reason you people should not relate to the matters of the household. Let people make whatever fuss they want in their houses; if a suspicion crosses your mind, stand at the door, say salam, and ask for permission. If permission is granted, go inside the house; otherwise, saying salam three times, return back.' As far as we know, Hazrat Umar not only did not issue any such directive, but numerous events are present in history books which are witness that his workers also kept spying, and Hazrat Umar himself spied to the extent that when breast-fed children wept in the nights, he would try to find out the cause of their weeping too.
The fourth thing is that if you believe this tradition, then it must be accepted that in an Islamic government, if some people drink wines in their houses, enjoy revelries with courtesans, heat up assemblies of dance and music, to the extent that they even gather stores of bombs and sten guns, even then the police of the government does not have the right to enter their houses and make their luxury bitter; to the extent that even the Caliph of the time does not have the right to dare to enter their houses without permission, and if ever by mistake he commits such daring, he should merely direct: 'In the future, you people should take caution from these kinds of matters.'
This tradition was not worthy of being addressed, but it has been presented with great confidence in this era by those people who read the litany of an Islamic government day and night, which is why it had to be addressed. In any case, this tradition is completely unreliable in our view. An Islamic government is the protector of the morals and character of the people as well, and is responsible for the peace, justice, and security of the country too. For this reason, it possesses the right that wherever any suspicion crosses its mind, it should spy on it. But on the exercise of every right, there are some moral and legal restrictions which the government also has to observe. If the government does not observe them, then although no one may be able to catch its hand, in the sight of Allah, those people will be wrongdoers who used a right to harass innocent individuals of the public which was granted to them for peace, justice, and the protection of the subjects.
Commendable Spying (Mustahsan Tajassus) 
As for common individuals, it is necessary for them to observe the following matters in this chapter:
That spying which is for the sake of someone's goodness, with a good intention and a good purpose, as we submitted under the verse, is not only not forbidden but is a highly righteous work. There is guidance in the Quran: for the help of the self-respecting poor, try to search for them and reach them yourself. Do not expect that they will cling to you and beg.
If regarding any person there is a suspicion that his hidden activities are a danger to the life, property, and honor of other innocent individuals, or damage to the peace, justice, and security of the country is expected from them, then one should not consider it a stranger's quarrel and stay detached from it.
